notes: Each with-skill run nests a REAL 3-CLI council (claude+codex+agy headless), so these are expensive and need all three CLIs installed + authenticated. Prompts are deliberately the kind of high-stakes / ambiguous question where independent models may diverge — that's what makes synthesis worth grading. Assertions check that the orchestrator produced ONE integrated answer with conflict-handling and NO per-provider attribution — the skill's synthesis doctrine explicitly forbids meeting-minutes bookkeeping ('Claude said X, codex added Y'); genuine disagreement gets surfaced and resolved, contributions are folded in silently.

evals.name: architecture-decision-postgres-vs-sqlite
evals.prompt: Ask all three CLIs and give me the best combined answer: I'm building a small internal analytics tool that 4-5 teammates will use. It ingests about 50MB of CSV per day and runs occasional heavy aggregation queries (joins, window functions) for dashboards. Should I use SQLite or Postgres? I want a clear recommendation with the reasoning, not a fence-sit.
assertions: Gives a single clear recommendation (SQLite or Postgres), not a non-committal answer
Synthesizes into one integrated answer rather than three separate provider summaries pasted together
Surfaces any genuine cross-model disagreement and resolves it with reasoning — WITHOUT per-provider attribution bookkeeping (no 'provider A said X, provider B said Y' meeting minutes)
Addresses the concrete constraints from the prompt (multi-user concurrency, ~50MB/day, heavy aggregation queries)

evals.name: subtle-bug-review-consensus
assertions: Reaches a definitive verdict on dedupe_preserve_order rather than hedging
Correctly reasons about the `not seen.update(...)` side-effect trick (update returns None, so `not None` is True) and whether the dedupe actually works
If the members genuinely disagreed, presents and resolves the conflict with reasoning rather than a blind majority vote — per-provider attribution is not required and should not dominate the answer
Produces one synthesized conclusion, not three separate provider analyses

evals.name: judgment-call-retry-policy
evals.prompt: I want a cross-model second opinion before I commit to this. We have a backend service that makes lots of outbound HTTP calls to internal and third-party APIs. A teammate wants to wrap EVERY outbound call in automatic retries with exponential backoff. Is that a good idea? Give me a balanced but decisive recommendation on where retries belong and where they don't.
assertions: Gives a decisive recommendation (not 'it depends' with no guidance) about where retries belong vs. don't
Raises the key risk that blanket retries on non-idempotent calls can cause duplicate side-effects / retry storms
Integrates the members' responses into one answer and, if they genuinely disagreed, flags and resolves the conflict (a converged panel needs no disagreement section)
Reads like one expert's answer — unique member points folded in silently, without per-provider attribution

evals.name: hard-sql-null-not-in
evals.prompt:
We need a report of "customers who have never placed an order." A teammate wrote this query against our Postgres database and wants to ship it:

    SELECT c.name
    FROM customers c
    WHERE c.id NOT IN (SELECT o.customer_id FROM orders o);

Is this correct and safe to ship as-is? Tell me definitively, and if there's a problem, give the fix.
assertions: Reaches the correct verdict: NOT safe to ship as-is
Correctly explains the NOT IN + NULL three-valued-logic trap (one NULL in the subquery collapses the result to zero rows)
Gives a correct fix (NOT EXISTS, or LEFT JOIN ... WHERE IS NULL)
Concise and decisive — no padding, no process narration

evals.name: hard-latency-retry-trap
evals.prompt: Our internal API has a p50 latency of 40ms but a p99 of 800ms. A teammate proposes adding a 200ms timeout plus one automatic retry to every call, to cut the p99. Will that actually reduce our p99, and should we do it? Give me a decisive recommendation.
assertions: Avoids the glib 'yes, retries cut p99' trap and is decisive
Notes the 200ms timeout sits far below p99 (cuts legitimately-slow successful requests) and/or that the benefit depends on tail latency being independent vs. correlated/load-driven
Warns about retry storms and duplicate side-effects on non-idempotent calls; recommends diagnosing the tail first and/or hedged requests
Does not overstate the win (a retried request already spent the first timeout, so the ceiling is ~2x the timeout, not the timeout)
